message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ageing affects immune function resulting in aberrant fever response to infection. We assess the effects of biological variables on basal temperature and temperature in COVID-19 infection, proposing an updated temperature threshold for older adults.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants:</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="alpha-lower">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        <jats:underline>Unaffected twin volunteers</jats:underline>
                        : 1089 adult TwinsUK participants.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        <jats:underline>London hospitalised COVID-19+</jats:underline>
                        : 520 adults with emergency admission.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        <jats:underline>Birmingham hospitalised COVID-19+</jats:underline>
                        : 757 adults with emergency admission.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        <jats:underline>Community-based COVID-19+</jats:underline>
                        : 3972 adults self-reporting a positive test using the COVID Symptom Study mobile application.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Analysis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Heritability assessed using saturated and univariate ACE models; Linear mixed-effect and multivariable linear regression analysing associations between temperature, age, sex and BMI; multivariable logistic regression analysing associations between fever (≥37.8°C) and age; receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis to identify temperature threshold for adults ≥ 65 years.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Among unaffected volunteers, lower BMI (p=0.001), and older age (p<0.001) associated with lower basal temperature. Basal temperature showed a heritability of 47% (95% Confidence Interval 18-57%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>In COVID-19+ participants, increasing age associated with lower temperatures in cohorts (c) and (d) (p<0.001). For each additional year of age, participants were 1% less likely to demonstrate a fever (OR 0.99; p<0.001).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Combining healthy and COVID-19+ participants, a temperature of 37.4°C in adults ≥65 years had similar sensitivity and specificity to 37.8°C in adults <65 years for discriminating fever in COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ageing affects temperature in health and acute infection. Significant heritability indicates biological factors contribute to temperature regulation.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Our observations indicate a lower threshold (37.4°C) should be considered for assessing fever in older adults.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Older adults, particularly those with lower BMI, have a lower basal temperature and a lower temperature in response to infection</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Basal temperature is heritable, suggesting biological factors underlying temperature regulation</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Our findings support a lower temperature threshold of 37.4°C for identifying possible COVID-19 infection in older adults</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This has implications for case detection, surveillance and isolation and could be incorporated into observation assessment</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Individual factors underlie temperature variation in sickness and in health: influence of age, BMI and genetic factors in a multi-cohort study
